children.text: I had Tommy DeFrantz and [inaudible 00:00:04] and Jaamil Kosoko come to Boulder and one of the pieces I included was a piece by Dianne McIntyre, but it was so great to have them come. Actually, this is one of the other reasons I got excited about doing it again because I also realized when we were all in the room together, we were now talking about the future. We weren't so concerned about or at least they were even encouraging me to not be so concerned about translating so that we can fit into the cannon. Yes, we're capturing these choreographies and these stories and these histories, but there's so much just in the making. Instead of well now these black dancers, not the qualification, but just honoring the moment that includes, like you said, the culture, the background, [inaudible 00:01:09] making his work.

This is why he makes it. He doesn't make it ... You don't have to put it in the box of here is this black man, but it's like and of course you never stopped being an African American man, but it's just this is the work that he's making and there's something about our histories and our cultures and our backgrounds and our questions that includes all of this. I don't have to subtract it or take it out. Then, what about the future? Where are we going? Where are we headed? Where are we? I'm also really curious about how ... What's happening right now. What are we seeing also in the work of African American artists, and not just African American artists, but that's speaking to these times? Like you said, there is something happening right now.
In the last 10 years, how have you seen choreography shift? How have you seen the work of young choreographers shift? How have you seen your work shift in the last 10 years?

Well, I think we are seeing the results of many of the things that we were heading toward and now not only are we there, it's like that's a moving target. You head in a direction of access, say, or translation and now there is such a move beyond what we thought the cannon was, the cannon that was being translated, that it has recreated itself. It has engendered a whole other level that maybe we didn't see coming. We forget that this an evolving form in step with its times. Not only there to reaffirm a way of thinking that had been very important for many people. Now, we're kind of the nephews of those very important nephews and nieces of those very important people are now making and looking and like hey, I think I'm interested in this direction.

I just did a thought that in my company's current work we've been ... One of the things that came up in the research for it was a conversation between Toni Morrison and Charlie Rose about paradise that she wrote in the late '90s, 98, 97, or so, where she made a very particular decision not identify the characters by race. She just said they are who they are and they know who they're talking to, so they don't have to say black person who I'm talking to. It's there. It's intrinsic. The reader then is there discovering from any vantage point who these people are, not through the assumed lens of these are black characters and so on and so forth. I think that in a way we are, what is this 20 years later, having absorbed that information in our lesson.

Younger choreographers are just yeah, okay, there's no assumption about who I'm talking to, what I have to say, but rather I'm following, I'm able to follow my interest, which can be in so many directions about honing in and what is the black experience or just honing in what is the human, what is the female experience, what is the experience, and maybe even working on something that has less to do with a human experience but just what are these angles and dynamics and extractions that are surrounding me.
Yes. I think we did determine it's been ...
Yeah.

Since we worked together or I learned Rain. Well, one question, how do Rain versus when you saw it 15 years ago and also since when you made it, I think, in 1983 or something like that? Do you see it any differently? Then you also performed it, do you experience it any differently? Do you?

It is ... I do. Yes. It'd be hard to pinpoint exactly what that difference is. One was an obvious just revisiting something that was made 30 years ago in a body that's now 30 years older. Working with you, alongside you and it's so interesting to still feel the impulses even I can't quite accomplish the moment brings back for me something about how it works in time, it works in its own musicality. It works in a musicality of a composed sound. I say that realizing that dancing to music is there's nothing new there, but rehearing the music of the dance in the body I feel is new for me. I feel like I'm rehearing what a body has to go through, not only in terms of its alignment with the music, but a sensation in time and how the crafting of that sensation in time was what I was doing 30 years ago, what I then aim to do 15 years ago and now have another opportunity to refine that. I should say that it's not so much to bring it back to what it was, but to really be true to the sequent tension and releases that it came from in order to ... Just not re-experiencing it, but there's something about that ordering of sensation that I feel is at the heart of the piece in a way that I wouldn't have been able to articulate 15 years ago or even 30 years ago. I hope that makes sense. No.
It's okay. It was fine. It just if you moved just a certain amount, it makes a little noise. No. you continue move. It just pulls.
Okay.
That's all.
Okay. I don't have to repeat that, do I?
I would've stopped you if it was bad.
Okay.
I just want to prevent it from rubbing on your skin.
Okay. Yeah. Can I say one more other thing about that if I can get it out?
Yeah.

Maybe this is in line with what I was saying earlier about younger dancers really stepping on the shoulders not only of their teachers and stuff, but just the shoulders have moved. It's all evolving. I feel for me as 30 years older, the gift of reentering a sensibility that was intuitive and now I have tools to question that or to just really what was I thinking or what was I really feeling and how does that lodge in a body, not only my body so that that is the information. I'm looking ultimately you are the holder of an event. It's not my event at all anymore, but it's like I want as best as possible for your experience in the time of that piece to be as generative for you.
You mentioned that the other day this idea of enjoying seeing that the ... I don't know if you said form.
Yeah.
It exists. It can exist on another body. It's not just like that can be passed on or that can be crafted in a way. It exists. It doesn't have to just be on my body or I can see it.

Yeah. The piece is a form. It sounds so abstract, but it carries itself and the work is fitting a whole other sensibility into that form so that your sensibility carries it to the next place. The form of the piece is what survives, not it's reference to me or to you but ultimately to deliver the piece to the next audience. We've talked about that place in the music where we both go like we kind of want to cry a moment. It's just so you get to sit on the grass and you get to just be there and how exquisite that feeling is. Not exquisite choreography, but just that feeling is exquisite and ultimately that is what an audience is getting. We are just in service to that moment on a good day. Yeah.
